evidence_quotes.quote: the publisher close the servers and now the products that consumers bought is not accessible anymore.
buying in this context does not give you ownership of the product but simply grants you a licence for the product that can be revoked in parts or all

paragraphs.text: Hello, I would like to suggest to take a closer look at video games as they are, as of right now, a wild west situation. First, there are hundreds of Video games that people have bought and after a few years/months or recently even weeks ("Concorde" was removed after two weeks) the publisher close the servers and now the products that consumers bought is not accessible anymore. Publishers like to play word tricks in their terms and conditions where they state nonsense like "buying in this context does not give you ownership of the product but simply grants you a licence for the product that can be revoked in parts or all" wanting to redefine the word BUY meaning that even if I buy a video game digitally I don't own it and that doesn't even take into account the thousands of customers who buy a physical copy and face the same issue even though they have not agreed to that nonsense. There is currently a European Citizens' Initiative called "Stop Destroying Videogames" with over 1.4 Million signatures that aims to bring this issue to parliament. Since this would directly expand the Digital Fairness Act I thought it would be worth to bring up here as well. Secondly, there is currently pressure from American online payment providers (Visa and MasterCard) who force online sellers to comply with what they deem as okay. As an example, the online platform Steam has recently removed hundreds of games that Visa and MasterCard deemed "risky", in fear of loosing these two payment providers for their customers, even though these games are legal to buy in the EU just because they didn't like what's presented within those games. Another example is the Japanese website DLsite, selling mature books, art, video games and videos was forced to close because Visa and MasterCard removed their payment processing from their site. I understand a lot of people having issues with nudity but that doesn't mean it should affect what I can buy or not, if I want to buy a painting of a naked man I am legally allowed to do so, yet these puritan companies force their will onto everyone and I do not think it is wise to let them decide what we can and cannot buy online we all know censorship starts with nudity and ends with a march of "Brownshirts". Best Regards, Jeremias Esberger - Austria
